title: Step 2: Run the following command to query your collection:

sections.heading: Overview
sections.body:
With a simple configuration change, you can enable semantic search, [Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with MongoDB](avs-rag), and memory for AI Agents, without writing embedding code,
managing model infrastructure, or handling vector pipelines. That is, when
[self-managed](manual/administration/install-community), `mongot`,
you can provide the [Voyage AI API (Application Programming Interface) Keys](voyage-api-key-for-auto-embed)
to use for generating embeddings, ideally one for indexing operations and
another for query operations from different projects.

After deployment:

- From your collection, choose the text field for which you want to enable
  the semantic search.

- From the list of available embedding models, choose an embedding model.

- In your MongoDB Vector Search index definition, configure Automated Embedding using
  the `autoEmbed` type.

To learn more about configuring MongoDB Vector Search index for Automated Embedding, see
[Index a Text Field](avs-index-for-auto-embed).

MongoDB Vector Search automatically generates embeddings for existing and new documents
that you insert or update by using the API (Application Programming Interface) keys that you specified while
initializing MongoDB Community Edition.

> **Note:** The generated embeddings are stored in a separate system collection on
> the same cluster.

For querying, use the `query.text` option in the [$vectorSearch](pipe.$vectorSearch)
pipeline stage. MongoDB Vector Search generates embeddings for the text query using the
same embedding model in the index definition. You can specify a different
embedding model using the `model` option in the [$vectorSearch](pipe.$vectorSearch)
pipeline stage, but the specified embedding model must be compatible with
the embedding model used at index-time. MongoDB Vector Search uses the query API key that
you provided during MongoDB Community initialization to generate the
embeddings at query-time. To learn more, see [Run a Text Query](avs-auto-embed-run-query).

You will incur charges for generating embeddings using the API (Application Programming Interface) keys. To
learn more, see [Costs](costs-for-auto-embed).

To automate embeddings and run a sample query, see [Get Started](auto-embed-get-started).

sections.heading: Voyage AI API (Application Programming Interface) Keys
sections.body:
While you can use a single API (Application Programming Interface) key for generating embeddings at
index-time and at query-time, we recommend that you use separate API (Application Programming Interface) keys
to avoid query operations from negatively impacting indexing operations.

You can generate API (Application Programming Interface) keys in the following ways:

- (**Recommended**) [using](voyageai/management/api-keys) your Atlas account,
  which allows you to manage your Voyage AI embedding model API (Application Programming Interface) key from the
  Atlas UI.

  To learn more about generating and managing API (Application Programming Interface) keys including
  [configuring the rate limits](voyageai/management/rate-limits) (which is a
  combination of TPM (Tokens Per Minute) and RPM
  (Requests Per Minute)) and [monitoring API key usage](voyageai/management/monitor-usage), see [Model API Keys](voyageai/management/api-keys).

- [Voyage AI](https://www.voyageai.com/).

  To learn more about managing the API (Application Programming Interface) keys created from Voyage AI, see
  .

After creating the keys, you must specify the keys you want to use for
automated embedding when configuring `mongot` during [self-managed](manual/administration/install-community) with MongoDB Community Edition. MongoDB Vector Search
uses the Voyage AI API (Application Programming Interface) key that you provided during deployment of
`mongot` to automatically generate embeddings for your data at index-
and for your query text at query-time.

sections.heading: Supported Embedding Models
sections.body:
MongoDB Vector Search integrates with Voyage AI's state-of-the-art embedding models,
each optimized for specific use-case:

| Embedding Model | Description                                                 |
| --------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| voyage-4-lite   | Optimized for high-volume, cost-sensitive applications.     |
| voyage-4        | (Recommended) Balanced performance for general text search. |
| voyage-4-large  | Maximum accuracy for complex semantic relationships.        |
| voyage-code-3   | Specialized for code search and technical documentation.    |

sections.heading: Costs
sections.body:
Embedding model pricing is usage-based, with charges billed to the
account linked to the API (Application Programming Interface) key used for access. Pricing is based on the
number of tokens in your text field and queries.

> **Note:** In the context of embedding models and LLMs, tokens are the
> fundamental units of text, such as words, subwords, or characters,
> that the model processes to create embeddings or generate text.
> Tokens are how you are billed for usage of embedding models and LLMs.

If you use the API (Application Programming Interface) key that you created using your Atlas account,
you can monitor API (Application Programming Interface) key usage from the Atlas UI. To learn more, see
[Manage Billing for the Embedding and Reranking API](voyageai/management/billing).

If you generated the API (Application Programming Interface) key directly from Voyage AI, see
 to learn more about the charge for
requests to the embedding service endpoint.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
While in Preview, Automated Embedding functionality is not yet available
for the following deployment types:

- Atlas clusters

- Local Atlas deployments using the Atlas CLI

- MongoDB Enterprise Edition

The feature is only available for MongoDB Search and MongoDB Vector Search [self-managed](manual/administration/install-community)
and for [deployments](kubernetes/fts-vs-deployment) using the MongoDB
Controllers for Kubernetes Operator with MongoDB 8.2 or later Community
Edition.

sections.heading: Get Started
sections.body:
Use the following tutorial to learn how to configure MongoDB Vector Search to
automatically generate vector embeddings. Specifically, you perform the
following actions:

- Index the field or fields in your collection that contain text data
  for which you want to automatically generate embeddings at index-time.

- Run a text query against the indexed fields using automatically
  generated embeddings at query-time.

This tutorial uses the [sample\_airbnb](sample-airbnb) namespace to demonstrate how to index a text field,
`summary`, in the collection for automatically generating embeddings
at index-time and run a text query against the indexed field,
`summary`, using embeddings generated at query-time.

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body:
To complete this tutorial, you must have the following:

- Self-managed deployment of MongoDB v8.2 or later Community Edition
  with MongoDB Search and MongoDB Vector Search

  To learn more, see [MongoDB Vector Search Quick Start](vector-search-quick-start).

- Valid Voyage AI API (Application Programming Interface) key or keys

  To learn more, see [Voyage AI API (Application Programming Interface) Keys](voyage-api-key-for-auto-embed).

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body: - MongoDB Compass

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body: - [mongosh](mongodb-shell)

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body: - [Go driver](go) v2.1 or later

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body: - [Java Sync driver](java/sync) v5.7 or later

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body: - [Node.js driver](node) v7.0 or later

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body: - [PyMongo driver](pymongo) v4.16 or later

sections.heading: Prerequisites
sections.body: - [.NET/C# driver](csharp) v3.6 or later

sections.heading: Index a Text Field
sections.body:
The index definition that you create in this tutorial indexes the
following fields in the `sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews`
collection:

- `summary` field as the `autoEmbed` type to automatically
  generate embeddings for the text data in the field using the
  `voyage-4` embedding model.

- `address.country` field as the `filter` type to prefilter the
  data for the semantic search using the string value in the field.

- `bedroom` field as the `filter` type to prefilter the data for
  the semantic search using the numeric value in the field.

To create this index:

sections.heading: Index a Text Field
Index a Text Field
sections.body:
To learn more, see [MongoDB Shell](connect-mongo-shell).

> **Example:** **Input:**
>
> ```shell
> use sample_airbnb
> ```
>
> **Output:**
>
> ```shell
> switched to db sample_airbnb
> ```

To create the index named `vector_index`, run the following
`db.collection.createSearchIndex()` method:

```javascript
db.listingsAndReviews.createSearchIndex(
  "vector_index",
  "vectorSearch", 
  {
    "fields": [
      {
        "type": "autoEmbed",
        "modality": "text",
        "path": "summary",
        "model": "voyage-4"
      },
      {
        "type": "filter",
        "path": "address.country"
      },
      {
        "type": "filter",
        "path": "bedrooms"
      }
    ]
  }
);
```

sections.heading: Index a Text Field
Index a Text Field
Index a Text Field
Index a Text Field
sections.body:
To define a MongoDB Vector Search index:

- Create a `.py` file.

  ```shell
  touch vector_index.py
  ```

- Copy and paste the following index definition in to the file.

  ```python
  from pymongo.mongo_client import MongoClient
  from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel
  import time

  # Connect to your deployment
  uri = "<connectionString>"
  client = MongoClient(uri)

  # Access your database and collection
  database = client["sample_airbnb"]
  collection = database["listingsAndReviews"]

  # Create your index model, then create the search index
  search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
      "fields": [
        {
          "type": "autoEmbed",
          "modality": "text",
          "path": "summary",
          "model": "voyage-4"
        },
        {
          "type": "filter",
          "path": "address.country"
        },
        {
          "type": "filter",
          "path": "bedrooms"
        }
      ]
    },
    name="vector_index",
    type="vectorSearch"
  )

  result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
  print("New search index named " + result + " is building.")

  # Wait for initial sync to complete
  print("Polling to check if the index is ready. This may take up to a minute.")
  predicate=None
  if predicate is None:
    predicate = lambda index: index.get("queryable") is True

  while True:
    indices = list(collection.list_search_indexes(result))
    if len(indices) and predicate(indices[0]):
      break
    time.sleep(5)
  print(result + " is ready for querying.")

  client.close()
  ```

- Replace the `<connectionString>` in the index definition and
  save the file.

  To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

To create the index, run the following command:

```shell
python vector_index.py
```

codeBlocks.content:
from pymongo.mongo_client import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel
import time

# Connect to your deployment
uri = "<connectionString>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# Access your database and collection
database = client["sample_airbnb"]
collection = database["listingsAndReviews"]

# Create your index model, then create the search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
  definition={
    "fields": [
      {
        "type": "autoEmbed",
        "modality": "text",
        "path": "summary",
        "model": "voyage-4"
      },
      {
        "type": "filter",
        "path": "address.country"
      },
      {
        "type": "filter",
        "path": "bedrooms"
      }
    ]
  },
  name="vector_index",
  type="vectorSearch"
)

result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print("New search index named " + result + " is building.")

# Wait for initial sync to complete
print("Polling to check if the index is ready. This may take up to a minute.")
predicate=None
if predicate is None:
  predicate = lambda index: index.get("queryable") is True

while True:
  indices = list(collection.list_search_indexes(result))
  if len(indices) and predicate(indices[0]):
    break
  time.sleep(5)
print(result + " is ready for querying.")

client.close()

sections.heading: Index a Text Field

mkdir VectorSearch
cd VectorSearch
dotnet new console

sections.heading: Run a Text Query
sections.body:
The query in this tutorial does the following:

- Runs against the the indexed `summary` field in the
  `sample_airbnb.listingsAndReviews` collection.

- Prefilters the properties using the following criteria:

  - Properties that have `3` or more `bedrooms`.

  - Properties in the `country` named `United States`.

- Performs a semantic search for properties that are `close to
  amusement parks` using the embeddings automatically generated
  with the `voyage-4` embedding model. The query:

  - Considers up to `100` nearest neighbors.

  - Limits the results to `10` documents.

To run this query:

sections.heading: Run a Text Query
sections.body:
On the Database screen, click the `sample_airbnb`
database, then click the `listingsAndReviews` collection.

To run this query in MongoDB Compass:

- Click the Aggregations tab.

- Click \</> Text to switch to JSON (Javascript Object Notation) view.

- Copy and paste to run the following query:

  **Input:**

  ```javascript
  [
    {
      "$vectorSearch": {
        "index": "vector_index",
        "path": "summary",
        "filter": {
          "$and": [
            {
              "bedrooms": {  "$gte": 3  }
            },
            {  
              'address.country': {  
                '$in': ['United States']
              }  
            } 
          ]
        },
        "query": {
          "text": "close to amusement parks"
        },
        "model": "voyage-4",
        "numCandidates": 100,
        "limit": 10
      }
    },
    {
      "$project": {
        "_id": 0,
        "name": 1,
        "summary": 1,
        "address": 1,
        "price": 1,
        "bedrooms": 1,
        "score": { $meta: "vectorSearchScore" }
      }
    }
  ]
  ```

  **Output:**


sections.heading: Run a Text Query
Run a Text Query
Run a Text Query
Run a Text Query
Run a Text Query
Run a Text Query
Check Index Status
sections.body:
To check the status of your MongoDB Vector Search index, run the following command
after replacing the following placeholders:

| \<connection-string> | Your cluster connection string.                             |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| \<database-name>     | The name of the database the collection.                    |
| \<collection-name>   | The name of the collection for which you created the index. |

```javascript
mongosh <connection-string>/<database-name> --eval '
  db.<collection-name>.getSearchIndexes().forEach(printjson);
'
```

The preceding command connects to your cluster and for the specified
collection in the specified database, iterates through each index
on the collection and returns the index definition in JSON (Javascript Object Notation) format.

> **Example:** 
>
> Sample Command to Retrieve Indexes on the movies Collection
>
> **Input:**
>
> ```javascript
> mongosh "mongodb://localhost:27017/sample_mflix" --eval '
>   db.movies.getSearchIndexes().forEach(printjson);
> '
> ```
>
> **Output:**
>
> ```javascript
> {
>    id: '698d005f8bf34e5e6624d086',
>    name: 'vector_index',
>    type: 'vectorSearch',
>    status: 'READY',
>    numDocs: 21349,
>    latestDefinition: {
>       type: 'vectorSearch',
>       indexID: ObjectId('698d005f8bf34e5e6624d086'),
>       name: 'vector_index',
>       database: 'sample_mflix',
>       lastObservedCollectionName: 'movies',
>       collectionUUID: UUID('3920f806-ec08-46ae-a73e-8e1e191e85f5'),
>       numPartitions: 1,
>       fields: [
>          {
>          type: 'autoEmbed',
>          path: 'fullplot',
>          model: 'voyage-4',
>          modality: 'text'
>          }
>       ],
>       definitionVersion: 0,
>       definitionVersionCreatedAt: '2026-02-11T22:19:11Z',
>       indexFeatureVersion: 4
>    }
> }
> ```

codeBlocks.content:
{
   id: '698d005f8bf34e5e6624d086',
   name: 'vector_index',
   type: 'vectorSearch',
   status: 'READY',
   numDocs: 21349,
   latestDefinition: {
      type: 'vectorSearch',
      indexID: ObjectId('698d005f8bf34e5e6624d086'),

         }

      indexFeatureVersion: 4
   }
}

sections.heading: Verify Embeddings Were Generated
sections.body:
To verify that MongoDB Vector Search has generated embeddings for your indexed text
data, run the following command after replacing the `<connection-string>`
placeholder with your cluster connection string:

```javascript
mongosh <connection-string> --eval '
   print("Embeddings: " +
      db.getSiblingDB("__mdb_internal_search")
         .getCollectionNames()
         .filter(c => c.match(/^[0-9a-f]{24}$/))
         .map(c => db.getSiblingDB("__mdb_internal_search").getCollection(c).countDocuments())
         .reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0)
   );
'
```

The preceding command connects to your cluster and returns the total
number of vector embeddings stored across all collections in your
deployment.

> **Example:** 
>
> Sample Command to Retrieve Embeddings Count
>
> **Input:**
>
> ```javascript
> mongosh "mongodb://localhost:27017/" --eval '
>    print("Embeddings: " +
>       db.getSiblingDB("__mdb_internal_search")
>          .getCollectionNames()
>          .filter(c => c.match(/^[0-9a-f]{24}$/))
>          .map(c => db.getSiblingDB("__mdb_internal_search").getCollection(c).countDocuments())
>          .reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0)
>    );
> '
> ```
>
> **Output:**
>
> ```javascript
> Embeddings: 21349
> ```
